content.full_text:
(916) 322-5662 
Kenneth K. Moy 
Benner, Harris & MOY 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
September 14, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
2140 Shattuck Avenue, Suite 1006 
Berkeley, CA 
94704-1258 
Dear Mr. Moy: 
Re: 
Association of Bay Area 
Governments 
Advice Letter No. A-83-l93 
You have asked what the Association of Bay Area Governments 
(ABAG) can do to minimize the effects of the disqualification 
requirements of the Political Reform Act when one or more ABAG 
members have a conflict of interest with respect to individual 
items in the Bay Area Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP). 
The 
ABAG is one of the co-lead agencies which acts on the AQMP along 
with the Bay Area Air Quality Management District (BAAQMD) and 
the Metropolitan Transportation Commission. 
The approval of all 
three co-leaa agencies is required for the adoption of the 
AQMP. 
The AQMP sets priorities and goals for the achievement of 
air quality standards in the Bay Area, targeting specified 
industries or sources or pollution for regulation. 
If an individual ABAG member has a financial interest which 
may be affected by the AQMP, disqualification may be required by 
the Political Reform Act. 
Government Code Sections 87100 and 
87013 require a public official, such as an ABAG member, to 
disqualify himself from participating in any decision which will 
foreseeably have a material financial effect11 on any business 
entity in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or 
more, from which the official has received income of $250 or 
more in the preceding 12 months, or of which the official is an 
11 
"Material financial effect" is defined in 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18702. 


Kenneth K. Moy 
September 14, 1983 
Page 2 
officer, director or employee.ll 
Thus, for example, an ABAG 
member may be required to disqualify himself from participating 
in a decision on an individual item in the AQMP, if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that inclusion of the item in the plan 
will have a material financial effect on a company in which the 
official owns over $1,000 worth of stock. 
Because the AQMP may affect many industries in the Bay Area, 
there appears to be a significant chance that some ABAG members 
may be required to disqualify themselves as to some items in the 
plan. 
As a policy matter, the Fair Political Practices 
Commission wants to encourage maximum participation in 
governmental decisions by public officials, while at the same 
time insuring that disqualification takes place whenever it is 
required by law. 
If possible, we want to avoid situations in 
which a member of a public body must disqualify himself from all 
participation in general plan decisions or similar decisions 
because of a conflict as to one item in the plan. 
In accordance 
with this policy, we advised the BAAQMD that, if a member had a 
conflict as to a single item in the AQMP, the BAAQMD could take 
a final vote on that item first, with the financially interested 
member disqualifying himself. 
The disqualified member could 
then vote on approval of the entire plan, including the item 
that had previously been adopted (or excluding it if it had been 
rejected) • 
You have asked whether the ABAG can use a slightly different 
procedure to deal with conflicts of ABAG members. 
The procedure 
we suggested to the BAAQMD is not the only procedure an agency 
can employ to deal with conflicts by agency members as to 
individual items in general plans. 
Any system that permits the 
vote on the item involving the conflict to be isolated from the 
vote on the rest of the general plan may be used. 
In your 
letter, you suggest the following system: 
The entire AQMP will be submitted to ABAG's Executive 
Board, which has the power to approve the AQMP. 
The AQMP 
would be presented to each Executive Board member with the 
caveat that if a member has a conflict of interest with 
~I There may also other decisions as to which 
disqualification is required, such as those affecting real 
property in which an official has an interest. 
For a fuller 
discussion of disqualification see Government Code Sections 
87100, 87103, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700-18704, and the 
Commission's pamphlet on Conflicts of Interest (copy enclosed) • 


Kenneth K. MOY 
September 14, 1983 
Page 3 
regard to any of the individual measures set forth in the 
AQMP, then the member should vote on the overall AQMP and 
simultaneously state that he or she is abstaining from a 
vote on the individual measure which poses the potential 
conflict. 
When all votes have been tallied, any individual 
measure not receiving the requisite number of affirmative 
.. votes for approval will ei ther be deleted from the AQMP, or 
the entire AQMP will be referred back to staff for further 
study in light of the failure of that particular measure or 
measures. 
The system you suggest seems adequate to deal with the 
problem of conflicts on individual items in the AQMP, and may be 
used by the ABAG as an alternative to the system we suggested to 
the BAAQMD, with two conditions. 
First, a member with a 
conflict may not participate in discussions prior to the vote on 
the AQMP with respect to any item as to which he has a conflict 
of interest. 
Second, if any individual item in the AQMP is 
voted ~n separately, any member with a conflict as to that item 
must, of course, disqualify himself both from voting on and from 
participating in discussions on that item.l/ 
I trust this has answered your question. 
If you have any 
further questions, please do not hesitate to write for further 
advice. 
BAM:plh 
cc: 
Milton Feldstein 
Very truly yours, 
\ 
LI 
( 
j 
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief 
Legal Division 
i/ It is possible that a decision on an individual item 
in the AQMP could materially affect a member's financial 
interest in another item. 
This could happen, for example, if 
inclusion or deletion of an item could substantially affect the 
priority of another item in which the member has an interest, 
making it much less or much more likely that the member's 
interest would be materially affected. 
In such a situation, a 
member has a conflict of interest, and disqualification is 
required. 


FREDERIC C. BENNER 
ARTHUR HARRIS 
KENNETH K. MOY 
JOHN C. EVANS 
OF COUNSEL 
Barbara Milman 
Legal Division Chief 
LAW OFFICES 
;B E NNE R, H A R R ISS M 0 Y 
; 
~40 SHATTUCK AVENUE, SUITE 1006 
ilMBEllELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704-1258 
August 22, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
State of California 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Re: FPPC No. SI-82/13, BAAQMD 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
(415) 848-0630 
I am Legal Counsel to the Association of Bay Area Governments (ABAG). 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of August 17, 1983, I am writing on the above-
referenced topic. On July 22, 1983, I received a copy of the letter from Milton Feldstein, 
Air Pollution Control Officer of the Bay Area Air Quality Management District 
(BAAQMD) to Mr. Steven R. Crooks, Legal Counsel, Enforcement Division, FPPC, dated 
July 18, 1983. The BAAQMD letter responded to Mr. Crooks' letter dated July 6, 1983 to 
Mr. Feldstein and John Powell, Legal Counsel to BAAQMD (transmitted under Mr. Crooks' 
cover letter dated July 21, 1983). At page 2 of the BAAQMD letter, Mr. Feldstein asserts 
that if the policy stated in the FPPC letter is "mandatory", then it is "equally applicable 
to the Association of Bay Area Governments". 
The FPPC letter sets forth a procedure whereby the members of a policy 
making body of a public entity may vote on an "entire general plan that incorporates many 
individual measures, when the vote as to a particular measure might result in a conflict of 
interest violation" by a specific member of that body. Further, the FPPC letter states 
that the procedure enumerated therein is one the BAAQMD "must follow" to avoid 
conflict of interest violations in the future. 
I do not take exception to the FPPC's policies regarding the situations wherein 
a conflict of interest violation might arise. However, I am concerned about the implied 
exclusivity of the procedure outlined in the FPPC letter for resolving potential conflict of 
interest violations in the situation addressed. Therefore, I am requesting that you respond 
to the following questions: 
1. 
Do the FPPC's policy concerns regarding potential conflict of interest 
violations by members of a policy making body of a public entity as described 
in the FPPC letter of July 6, 1983, extend to 
to 
it 


Barbara Milman, Legal Division Chief 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 22, 1983 
Page 2 
ABAG is the last co-lead agency to act on the AQMP. Upon approval, the AQMP is 
forwarded to the Air Resource Board for its action. Does ABAG's role in the 
promulgation of the AQMP make it subject to the policy concerns raised by FPPC in its 
July 6, 1983 letter? 
ABAG has not adopted or utilized any particular procedure for segregating or 
identifying potential conflict of interest violations which may be caused by individual 
elements of the AQMP. To my knowledge, the situation has never arisen. However, in 
order to safeguard against a possible unknowing violation, it seems appropriate that a 
procedure be adopted to identify such potential violations and to avoid them by having the 
individual policy maker abstain from voting on a particular measure. However, the 
procedure outlined in the FPPC letter is ill suited to the manner in which ABAG actually 
adopts the AQMP. Therefore, ABAG staff and I recommend the following procedure. 
The entire AQMP will be submitted to ABAG's Executive Board, which has the 
power to approve the AQMP. The AQMP would be presented to each Executive Board 
member with the caveat that if a member has a conflict of interest with regard to any of 
the individual measures set forth in the AQMP, then the member should vote on the 
overall AQMP and simultaneously state that he or she is abstaining from a vote on the 
individual measure which poses the potential conflict. When all votes have been taWed, 
any individual measure not receiving the requisite number of affirmative votes for 
approval will either be deleted from the AQMP, or the entire AQMP will be referred back 
to staff for further study in light of the failure of that particular measure or measures. Is 
the preceding procedure adequate in light of the issues raised in the FPPC letter of 
July 6, 1983? 
Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. 
KKM:lda

sections.question:
You have asked what the Association of Bay Area Governments (ABAG) can do to minimize the effects of the disqualification requirements of the Political Reform Act when one or more ABAG members have a conflict of interest with respect to individual items in the Bay Area Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP).

You have asked whether the ABAG can use a slightly different procedure to deal with conflicts of ABAG members. The procedure we suggested to the BAAQMD is not the only procedure an agency can employ to deal with conflicts by agency members as to individual items in general plans. Any system that permits the vote on the item involving the conflict to be isolated from the vote on the rest of the general plan may be used. In your letter, you suggest the following system: The entire AQMP will be submitted to ABAG's Executive Board, which has the power to approve the AQMP. The AQMP would be presented to each Executive Board member with the caveat that if a member has a conflict of interest with regard to any of the individual measures set forth in the AQMP, then the member should vote on the overall AQMP and simultaneously state that he or she is abstaining from a vote on the individual measure which poses the potential conflict. When all votes have been tallied, any individual measure not receiving the requisite number of affirmative votes for approval will either be deleted from the AQMP, or the entire AQMP will be referred back to staff for further study in light of the failure of that particular measure or measures. Is the preceding procedure adequate in light of the issues raised in the FPPC letter?
sections.conclusion: The system you suggest seems adequate to deal with the problem of conflicts on individual items in the AQMP, and may be used by the ABAG as an alternative to the system we suggested to the BAAQMD, with two conditions. First, a member with a conflict may not participate in discussions prior to the vote on the AQMP with respect to any item as to which he has a conflict of interest. Second, if any individual item in the AQMP is voted on separately, any member with a conflict as to that item must, of course, disqualify himself both from voting on and from participating in discussions on that item.

Kenneth K. MOY 
September 14, 1983

AQMP, then the member should vote on the overall AQMP and 
simultaneously state that he or she is abstaining from a 
vote on the individual measure which poses the potential 
conflict. 
When all votes have been tallied, any individual 
measure not receiving the requisite number of affirmative 
.. votes for approval will ei ther be deleted from the AQMP, or 
the entire AQMP will be referred back to staff for further 
study in light of the failure of that particular measure or 
measures. 
The system you suggest seems adequate to deal with the 
problem of conflicts on individual items in the AQMP, and may be 
used by the ABAG as an alternative to the system we suggested to 
the BAAQMD, with two conditions. 
First, a member with a 
conflict may not participate in discussions prior to the vote on 
the AQMP with respect to any item as to which he has a conflict 
of interest. 
Second, if any individual item in the AQMP is 
voted ~n separately, any member with a conflict as to that item 
must, of course, disqualify himself both from voting on and from 
participating in discussions on that item.l/ 
I trust this has answered your question. 
If you have any 
further questions, please do not hesitate to write for further 
advice. 
BAM:plh 
cc: 
Milton Feldstein
sections.question_synthetic: Can ABAG use an alternative procedure to handle conflicts of interest by its members on individual items in the Air Quality Management Plan, whereby members vote on the overall plan while simultaneously abstaining from voting on specific measures where they have conflicts?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC approves ABAG's proposed alternative procedure for handling conflicts on individual AQMP items, provided that members with conflicts abstain from pre-vote discussions on those items and must fully disqualify themselves if any conflicted item is voted on separately.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter (August 22, 1983) from Kenneth K. Moy and FPPC response letter (September 14, 1983) from Barbara A. Milman. Extracted as advice_letter with focus on FPPC's response. Question and conclusion sections identified from FPPC response portion. Clear policy guidance provided with specific conditions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked what the Association of Bay Area Governments (ABAG) can do to minimize the effects of the disqualification requirements of the Political Reform Act when one or more ABAG members have a conflict of interest with respect to individual items in the Bay Area Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP).

You have asked whether the ABAG can use a slightly different procedure to deal with conflicts of ABAG members. The procedure we suggested to the BAAQMD is not the only procedure an agency can employ to deal with conflicts by agency members as to individual items in general plans. Any system that permits the vote on the item involving the conflict to be isolated from the vote on the rest of the general plan may be used. In your letter, you suggest the following system: The entire AQMP will be submitted to ABAG's Executive Board, which has the power to approve the AQMP. The AQMP would be presented to each Executive Board member with the caveat that if a member has a conflict of interest with regard to any of the individual measures set forth in the AQMP, then the member should vote on the overall AQMP and simultaneously state that he or she is abstaining from a vote on the individual measure which poses the potential conflict. When all votes have been tallied, any individual measure not receiving the requisite number of affirmative votes for approval will either be deleted from the AQMP, or the entire AQMP will be referred back to staff for further study in light of the failure of that particular measure or measures. Is the preceding procedure adequate in light of the issues raised in the FPPC letter?

CONCLUSION: The system you suggest seems adequate to deal with the problem of conflicts on individual items in the AQMP, and may be used by the ABAG as an alternative to the system we suggested to the BAAQMD, with two conditions. First, a member with a conflict may not participate in discussions prior to the vote on the AQMP with respect to any item as to which he has a conflict of interest. Second, if any individual item in the AQMP is voted on separately, any member with a conflict as to that item must, of course, disqualify himself both from voting on and from participating in discussions on that item.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-193 approves ABAG's proposed procedure for managing potential conflicts of interest when Executive Board members vote on the Air Quality Management Plan, allowing members to vote on the overall plan while abstaining from specific measures where they have financial conflicts, subject to conditions regarding pre-vote discussions and separate votes.
